title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] SHOBANA RADHAKRISHNAN:
Hi, I'm Shobana, senior director of
engineering for TV at Google. Today we'll dive into
the latest updates from Google TV and our
underlying Android TV OS and explore the ways you
can build excellent TV apps. Last fall, we mentioned
that Google TV and Android TV achieved over 270 million
monthly active devices, establishing one of the
largest smart TV OS footprints. We look forward to
increasing this momentum by bringing our platform
to more form factors and delivering more delightful
user experiences this year. We are glad to see users
interact with our platform across a diverse range of
entry-level to premium devices, from TVs, phones, and tablets to
projectors and, most recently, smart monitors. We're especially proud to
see support for Google TV on the awarded OMEN 32x smart
gaming monitor and have our new Google TV Streamer recognized as
the best media streaming device by the "New York
Times Wirecutter." Today, we are excited
to share even more ways users can access effortless
entertainment on Google TV this year. First off, we'll be
partnering with select OEMs to launch affordable and
reliable low-RAM panel TVs, allowing more
budget-conscious users to experience quality
entertainment. We are also excited to soon
bring Google TV to headsets, powered by Android XR. Users can see, hear,
and explore the action from the best seat in the house. They can access their
favorite content across their subscriptions,
search for content, and get curated recommendations. Our broad platform availability
across a variety of form factors directly translates to
more opportunities for you to connect with users worldwide. To further enrich
the user experience, we brought more apps
to our global platform. Just to name a few,
we have launched HISTORY Vault in the US,
BBC, Channel 4, ARD, TF1, and ITV in EMEA, as well as
JioHotstar and Vidio in the APAC region. Now I can watch my
favorite Bollywood movies from these apps on Google TV. Our quality streaming
partnerships
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA

HISTORY Vault in the US,
BBC, Channel 4, ARD, TF1, and ITV in EMEA, as well as
JioHotstar and Vidio in the APAC region. Now I can watch my
favorite Bollywood movies from these apps on Google TV. Our quality streaming
partnerships also allow us to sustain
strong app engagement. In 2024, US users spent
approximately 200 minutes in apps per day. As our platform becomes
even more diversified with excellent apps, we're
also making improvements to help users find what to watch
and offer new ways for your apps to reach your target users. Last year, we previewed
the Video Discovery API that's available for partners. Video discovery optimizes
resumption, entitlements, and recommendations across
all Google TV form factors to enhance the end
user experience and boost app engagement. You can now easily
display user's post video within the Continue Watching
row from the home screen. Resumption drives 60% of all
user interactions on Google TV. This interface allows
users to resume viewing from where they left
off across any device when they are signed in
with their Google account. The Video Discovery
API also streamlines entitlement management, which
matches content to eligibility. This eligibility might be
from a subscription, purchase, or a free ad-supported model. Video discovery allows users
to share their entitlements through one simple
opt-in process. Users appreciate this because
they can enjoy personalized recommendations without
needing to manually update all their subscription details. This allows you,
as our partners, to connect with users across
multiple discovery points on Google TV. That means more immediate access
and increased app engagement. The Video Discovery API even
highlights personalized content recommendations based on content
that users watch inside apps. Partners can begin incorporating
the Video Discovery API today, starting with resumption
and entitlement integrations. Check out the link for
more details to enroll. Google TV is committed
to responsibly using AI
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA
text:
Partners can begin incorporating
the Video Discovery API today, starting with resumption
and entitlement integrations. Check out the link for
more details to enroll. Google TV is committed
to responsibly using AI to bring more helpfulness
into the home too. For instance, last fall, we
released AI-generated content descriptions which help users
find their next favorite film or series to watch. Users can access bite-sized
season summaries, learn more about a movie, and
see feedback from other viewers. Google TV also offers
custom AI screensavers. So users can simply
use their voice to create stunning
screensavers for the largest screen in their home. Earlier this year, we announced
that we'll bring Gemini capabilities to Google TV. So users can speak
more naturally and conversationally to find
what to watch and get answers to complex questions. Our new AI assistant
now understands users' natural,
conversational voice searches. It then analyzes titles from
your apps and shares tailored recommendations, effectively
boosting the visibility of your app's content. Let's dive deeper into
what this looks like. For instance, I want to share
my interest in the classic "Jurassic Park" movies
with my younger kids, but I don't want to show
them something too intense. Now I can easily ask questions
like, I like Jurassic park. What are some movies like it
that I can watch with my kids? And voila, I get
personalized recommendations, perfect for my
next family night. Plus, users can
naturally ask questions to explore their
latest curiosities and get insightful answers
with supporting videos for deeper learning. They can invite the whole family
to learn, asking questions like, can you explain the solar
system to my first grader? And the AI assistant will
provide a personalized response with suitable video
suggestions that a first grader would be excited to see. When we demoed Google
TV with Gemini at CES, we were pleased to receive the
best AI award from Tom's Guide. We are so excited to bring this
new, helpful, and delightful
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA
text:
with suitable video
suggestions that a first grader would be excited to see. When we demoed Google
TV with Gemini at CES, we were pleased to receive the
best AI award from Tom's Guide. We are so excited to bring this
new, helpful, and delightful experience to users this fall. There's never been a better
time to be a Google TV partner or developer. I'll now hand it over to Paul
from our developer relations team, who will share more
about Compose for TV, provide suggestions on
improving your apps performance, and explore some exciting
features from Android 16. Over to you, Paul. PAUL LAMMERTSMA:
Thanks, Shobana. Last year, we launched
Compose for TV, which lets you build beautiful,
adaptive UIs across Android, including Android TV OS. If your app is following best
practices for adaptive apps, you've done most of the work
already to adapt to TVs. Compose is also where
we're continually improving the developer experience. SoundCloud built upon
these improvements to overhaul their apps
design system with Compose, building new features and
using 45% less code on average. It took them just four
months to build their TV app from the ground up. You can read more about their
Compose journey on the Android developer blog. Compose for TV 1.0 is stable and
expands on the core and material Compose libraries. The March 2025
release of Compose significantly
improves app startup in our internal benchmarking
sample on mobile, with roughly a 20% improvement
compared with the March 2024 release. We've seen jank rates on older
Pixel 3A devices reduced by up to 76%. Both of these metrics are
already considerably better than views performance. And on hardware-constrained
devices like TVs, those enhancements are huge. Our updated Jetcaster
sample demonstrates how to use Compose
across form factors. It includes a dedicated module
for playing podcasts on TV by combining separate view
models with shared business logic. Check out the details in the
Compose tips for delightful UI video series. We've now also updated
our JetStream sample
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA

It includes a dedicated module
for playing podcasts on TV by combining separate view
models with shared business logic. Check out the details in the
Compose tips for delightful UI video series. We've now also updated
our JetStream sample to demonstrate how to share
logic within a single module. This approach uses
the most amount of code between form factors. These are two
comprehensive samples that show how to reduce code
and maintenance by building adaptive apps for Android. We've also heard your
feedback about handling focus and are making iterative
improvements, including our newly published CodeLab. We've seen three
common challenges when handling focus
behavior on TV-- first, setting initial focus. Second, taking over control
of unexpected focus traversal. And third, restoring focus. Let's explore setting
initial focus. In a TV UI, whenever
opening a screen, the initial focus
needs to be set. Compose for TV does this
in the majority of cases, but sometimes the default might
differ from your preference. You can control that initial
focus in three steps. First, declare a
focus requester that's wrapped in a member function. Then apply a focus requester
modifier on the first card. And finally, call the
request focus method in the launched effects
composable function. This last bit ensures
that the focus is moved to the right element
when the composable is composed for the first time. Now let's take a look
at some scenarios where focus moves to an
unexpected component. Here, we have four items
arranged in rows and columns. When I press the
right D-pad button, the focus moves from the
first item to the third item. That's odd. To understand why
this is happening, we can look at our
layout hierarchy. The four focus targets
are at the same level, and ThirdCard is the
first item to the right of FirstCard in the layout. That's why focus
moves from the first to the third with the
right directional key. We can resolve this
by creating a focus group that consists of the
focus targets inside the row.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA

of FirstCard in the layout. That's why focus
moves from the first to the third with the
right directional key. We can resolve this
by creating a focus group that consists of the
focus targets inside the row. Now, since the row
and the first card are the only focus
targets at the same level, pressing the right
directional key no longer moves to the third card
from within a focus group. We should also make sure
that focus is restored when the user returns to it. By using the focus
restore modifier, we can make sure
that the focus is brought to the right
element in the group. Now, these are
just a few examples taken straight from our new
focus management CodeLab. Check the video description
for an entire guide. Our goal is to make
this even easier, and we'll continue to improve
focus behavior in Compose. So stay tuned to the Android
developer channel for updates later this year. Whether or not
you're using Compose, getting optimal performance
out of your TV app is always important. This is why we've published
a comprehensive guide on memory optimisation,
including memory targets for low-RAM devices. Combined with Android Studio's
powerful memory profiler, this helps you understand when
it exceeds those limits and why. In the worst case, if your app
is using memory carelessly, Android TV OS may perform
direct memory reclaim, a process that might
severely disrupt your app. But more often,
ineffective use of memory will trigger garbage
collection in your own app, resulting in a
jarring experience. I've pulled up an old leanback
app as an example of bitmap allocations and
how it makes memory grow bigger than necessary. It displays a typical
grid of images that are loaded dynamically, and oof. As you can see, it does
not have good performance. Let's take a peek at
the code that handles displaying the poster images. It has a very
straightforward approach using Glide to load
images into cards. OK, let me restart
the app and start a session of a memory profiler. I'm attaching it
to the app process

displaying the poster images. It has a very
straightforward approach using Glide to load
images into cards. OK, let me restart
the app and start a session of a memory profiler. I'm attaching it
to the app process and selecting the preset to
track memory allocations. Great, it started. While I scroll on
the screen, we can see how the Java memory
in blue and the graphics memory in orange are
growing simultaneously until a spike of
memory is reached. Ooh, look at those
little garbage can icons. They indicate the many times
garbage collector is called. And every single time, it's
caused a little bit of jank in my app. Also, notice how there's
a spike in the memory graph before garbage
collection kicked in? We want to prevent as
many spikes as possible. On this low-memory device,
it's preferential to avoid using two different
types of memory for images when we could
mostly use graphics memory. By loading the
images this way, I'm loading them into Java
memory and displaying them using graphics memory,
which is effectively using twice the amount. All right, back to the code. Let's change the
Glide configuration to use hardware-backed
bitmaps so we can transfer the bitmap
directly to the memory and free up our
Java memory space. We'll do this by changing
the request options and setting the Downsampler
allow hardware config to true. It'd be neater to define this
in our app configuration. But just for
illustration, I'm just going to drop this
line right here. Let's run the app again and
attach a new memory profiler session to it. Now, by doing the same type
of scrolling as before, we can now observe that
both types of memory do not grow simultaneously. This configuration
allows for the bitmaps to be released from
Java memory as they are loaded into graphics. So in general, less
memory is being consumed, and consequently, we're
seeing fewer spikes. Memory also frees up
sooner once we stop scrolling compared to before. I've been using a low-RAM
device for illustrating this optimization.

So in general, less
memory is being consumed, and consequently, we're
seeing fewer spikes. Memory also frees up
sooner once we stop scrolling compared to before. I've been using a low-RAM
device for illustrating this optimization. And as you can see, it's
essential to test your app on lower-end hardware. Now, we can use this as
it is, or we can set it up for only low-RAM devices. On higher-memory
devices, we may consider allowing Glide to use its bitmap
cache to rapidly display images instead of reading
them from disk. After all, unused
memory is wasted memory, and striking a good balance here
is really the name of the game. I can call
activityManager.isLowRAMDevice to check if there is
an low-RAM device. I need to instance of
the activity manager. And with that, I can check
for the low-RAM flag. Now let me move that
Glide configuration inside the condition,
and we're good to go. Using Compose in combination
with Coil's async image provides these kinds of memory
optimizations out of the box, but you should still be vigilant
about memory consumption. For example, if you're
loading bitmaps into a painter instead of directly
into a Composable, Coil will not be able to
detect how large it needs to be on the screen, and it
will always load the image with its original dimensions. You might also benefit from
deliberately downsampling images on low-RAM devices by
specifying a size modifier, like I've done here. This only scratches
the surface, so check the guide for more details
about optimizing for memory. The guide also covers
recommendations for reducing memory overhead
when working with graphics, media, and bindings. Examples include performing
image composition off the device and allocating the
video seq buffer. We're excited to announce the
upcoming release of Android 16 for TV. Developers can begin using
the latest beta today. Android 16 includes some great
features to help TV developers. First, the new media
quality API provides a standardized interface
to developers and vendors to manage picture profiles.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA
text:
Developers can begin using
the latest beta today. Android 16 includes some great
features to help TV developers. First, the new media
quality API provides a standardized interface
to developers and vendors to manage picture profiles. For movies and TV series that
are mastered with a wider dynamic range, developers
might request filmmaker mode to accurately display content
as the creator intended for it to look. A cinema profile with
greater color accuracy brings out subtle
details and shadows in favor of
increasing brightness. Conversely, live
sporting events, which are often mastered
with a narrow dynamic range and watched in
daylight, can benefit from a profile that gives
preference to brightness over color accuracy. If you'd like to understand
which profile a device running Android 16 or later supports,
query MediaQuality manager using getAvailablePictureProfiles. The profile ID may match against
a known system profile, some of which may differ by OEM. To apply a specific profile, you
can query MediaQuality manager by profile ID-- for example, for this
one for cinema profile. You can then apply
this picture profile as a parameter to mediaCodec
by providing in a parcelable into its bundle. Selecting a picture
profile in ExoPlayer will be available in a future
release later this year. You can find the list
of predefined profiles in the updated guide for
adjusting display settings. Note that these
presets aren't fixed. TVs come with some
preset profiles, but OEMs can include more,
and users can update them based on their preferences. Another new feature
in Android 16 is platform support
for Eclipsa audio. This is a new opensource
spatial audio codec based on the immersive audio
model and formats developed by Google, Samsung, and
other key contributors within the Alliance
for Open Media. It's designed to revolutionize
immersive audio experiences across a wide range
of applications, including streaming and gaming
in augmented or virtual reality. And because the
format is open source,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – OosLbRBM9dA
text:
within the Alliance
for Open Media. It's designed to revolutionize
immersive audio experiences across a wide range
of applications, including streaming and gaming
in augmented or virtual reality. And because the
format is open source, anyone can use
freely-available audio tools or leverage automated
workflows that use FFmpeg to create Eclipsa audio files. You can already begin using
these codecs today, even on older platforms, by using
the built-in support in Media3 ExoPlayer from version 1.5. By compiling the
Lib IAMF library, you'll get a dedicated
audio renderer instance. For details on
how to compile it, see the decoder extension
guide on GitHub. Android 16 for TV now
supports 64-bit kernels, which means you can run the
emulator natively on a 64-bit host machine. Although there aren't any
64-bit Android TV devices yet, it shows how we're laying
the engineering framework. We recommend that you also
begin preparing your TV apps for 64-bit compatibility,
just like you have been doing for other Android form factors. But it's important to continue
supporting 32-bit platforms for many years to come. And remember that you
can use app bundles to minimize your application
size by supporting both. Finally, let's explore
some more areas where developing for
Android TV OS brings consistency and delightful
experiences across Android. Let's start by taking a closer
look at content discovery. If you're a seasoned Android
developer for Android TV OS, the Continue Watching row
probably sounds familiar. And perhaps you
struggle to implement it because the Watch Next
API is transactional, requiring persistent
client-side IDs. You can now use the greatly
simplified Video Discovery API, which Shobana
showed us earlier. Since this API is powered
by the Engage SDK, it's simultaneously preparing
your content for more surfaces, like the recommendation
row, and across other Android-powered devices. First, we'll discover if
this API is available, depending on your
participation in the program.

it's simultaneously preparing
your content for more surfaces, like the recommendation
row, and across other Android-powered devices. First, we'll discover if
this API is available, depending on your
participation in the program. Learn more about enrolling
at the end of the session. For the code in
the next sections, let's assume that we've checked
it's available by calling isServiceAvailable. When using the
Video Discovery API, creating content entity
types is very similar. And you can collect them
into a continuation cluster. And here's the neat thing. Whenever you want to update
each entity in the cluster, all you need to do is
submit the entire cluster with a single call to
publish continuation cluster. We've heard your feedback. You don't have to provide
insertions, updates, or removals to individual entities anymore. To support device entitlements,
as Shobana showed earlier, you can associate
multiple account profiles to enable a personalized
Continue Watching experience. You can do this by providing
a record through Set Account Profile. And after getting
user consent, your app can then share the user's data
across all of their devices by calling
setSyncAcrossDevices with true. This is a phenomenal way to keep
your content recommendations in sync as users
watch your app's content on different devices. Like Shobana said, this API
is not just for TV either. You can use the appropriate
Engage SDK libraries for other form factors. And as a reminder, if you're not
enrolled in the Video Discovery API program and are
interested in joining, head over to this short
link to fill out the form. Last year, we showed a sneak
preview of the in-app ratings and reviews prompt for TV. I'm happy to announce that
the in-app ratings and reviews API is available for
all TV developers. By implementing the same Play
Core API you know and love on mobile, you can
convert delightful moments in your TV app into a higher
app rating on Google Play. For more details,
read the blog post that's linked in the
video description.

By implementing the same Play
Core API you know and love on mobile, you can
convert delightful moments in your TV app into a higher

read the blog post that's linked in the
video description. As you can see, these
updates bring consistency to app development
across Android, making it easier than ever
for developers like you to build apps that work
across all form factors and engage with users through
consistent APIs and surfaces. We're excited to see the
immersive, engaging experiences that you create. [MUSIC PLAYING]
